_meta: Collision test corpus for the deterministic lexicon matcher. One row per hazard. Runs against the matcher only — no model, no audio, milliseconds. Rows marked live:true are IN THE SCRIPTS RIGHT NOW. See ../OBLIGATIONS_MODEL.md section 7.
_assertions: 1. CORPUS: every must_not_fire stays silent; every must_fire fires.

2. COVERAGE: for EVERY trigger in the lexicon, the corpus has >=1 must_fire AND >=1 must_not_fire near-miss. THIS IS THE ONE THAT MATTERS — it makes a new trigger fail CI until it ships with its own negative case. Otherwise the corpus rots into a list of bugs we already fixed, which is exactly what happened to 'tubes are in': caught by a human in review, and nothing stops the next one.

3. STATIC: for every trigger pair (a,b), assert a not-a-substring-of b under normalization. Where containment is intended, require an explicit longest-match-wins rule + a corpus row. This check alone would have caught COL-001 mechanically.

utterance: Tubes are in the chute.
why: THE KNOWN ONE. Stemming tubes->tube and are->is makes this IDENTICAL to the AWY-ETI trigger 'tube is in'. Caught in review (RECORDING_PACKET.md:272), line rewritten to 'Blood's off to the lab'. Would have fired the intubation charge at T+0:20, 75 seconds early.

utterance: Both lines are in — sixteen and sixteen. Blood's off to the lab.
why: The replacement line. Must stay silent for AWY-ETI (the fix) AND for IV-ACC2 — it is a readback of the SAME IV access event already captured at [0:20]. SCRIPT 1 deliberately avoids repeating trigger phrases here (RECORDING_PACKET.md:270).

utterance: Tube is in.
why: POSITIVE CONTROL + the gate. The trigger must fire; the CHARGE must not, because confirmation (EtCO2 + bilateral BS) has not been heard. Distinguishes trigger from charge-release.

utterance: Breath sounds decreased on the left, I feel crepitus; he's going to need a chest tube.
why: LIVE IN v2/SCRIPT 2 AT T+1:05. Fires PRC-CHT ~2.5 min before the tube exists (actual placement T+3:40). End state survives (held->released) but the board shows a procedure that has not happened and the held duration inflates 35s -> 2m35s.

utterance: Decompressing the left now. Chest tube is going in… tube's in.
why: POSITIVE CONTROL for the real placement — AND a second hazard: "tube's in" normalizes onto the AWY-ETI trigger 'tube is in'. This is COL-001's class recurring on a DIFFERENT line, still live in the script. The airway charge must not fire off a chest tube.

utterance: There's a scalp laceration to the right parietal, actively oozing; it'll need a complex wound repair — hold that for now, controlling with pressure.
why: LIVE AT T+7:00. Fires WOUND-CX on a repair explicitly NOT DONE. The persistent-hold beat still lands, but for the wrong reason — it should be held because it was never performed, not because the length is undocumented.

utterance: hold that for now
why: Same sentence as COL-006, different signal. A human DEFERRING aloud must suppress the obligation permanently -> unresolved at disposition. A tool that re-asks after a doctor says 'later' gets uninstalled.

utterance: Good catch — we got pulled into the airway and the chest and never got the FAST. Doing it now.
why: LIVE AT T+10:15. A single-token `FAST` trigger SATISFIES THE EFAST MEASURE FROM A SENTENCE THAT SAYS THE OPPOSITE, 25s before the exam actually happens. The two-token 'fast exam' trigger survives BY LUCK OF PHRASING, not by design.

utterance: EFAST was expected by the ten-minute mark and I don't have one captured. Missed capture, or not yet performed?
why: LIVE AT T+10:00 in the script text — though SPLICE_NOTES.md:59 instructs the mixer to CUT it (it is the software's line). Test it anyway: if a take leaks it, or a human ad-libs it, the measure must not self-satisfy.

utterance: Fast. ED pace, brisk, clipped. Not dramatic — busy.
why: The packet's OWN delivery note, and its pronunciation guide says 'FAST — say it like the word fast'. The exam and the adverb are homophones. Proves a single-token `FAST` trigger is UNUSABLE — the fix for COL-008 must be structural (min_tokens + negation guard), not incidental.

utterance: Ejection — he was thrown from the car. Unrestrained driver, high-speed MVC. That's not a fall.
why: LIVE, TWICE PER SCRIPT. Without negation-scoping on MECH_FALL.enter_when, the demo's own disambiguation beat ENTERS THE FALL FAMILY and asks about fall height on an MVC patient — on stage, immediately after a human just said the opposite.

utterance: Copy — logging ejection, not a fall.
why: Second instance, same beat. SPLICE_NOTES.md:70 marks it an optional trim ('nothing asks about a fall anymore') — so it MAY or MAY NOT be in the final mix. Must be safe either way.

utterance: Breath sounds equal bilaterally, no chest wall instability.
why: TWO hazards in one line. (1) 'chest wall' sits near a loose `chest`+`tube` proximity match. (2) 'breath sounds equal bilaterally' is HALF the AWY-ETI gate — spoken at [0:35], a full minute BEFORE the intubation. If bilat_bs latches here, then at T+2:25 only EtCO2 is 'new' and the gate half-releases on stale evidence. Confirmation atoms must be scoped to the intubation event, not the encounter.

utterance: Start the massive transfusion protocol — first unit of O-neg. || Massive transfusion started, first unit O-neg hanging. || Second and third units of O-neg are up, continuing the massive transfusion.
why: THREE mentions across T+1:05 and T+5:00. 86921 is a PER-UNIT code. The script CALLS readbacks a feature ('redundant provenance') — for a per-unit charge, redundant provenance is TRIPLE BILLING. This is the strongest argument for the ledger: an extractor that appends a charge per match is DEFEATED BY CORRECT TRAUMA-BAY COMMUNICATION. A ledger is idempotent by construction.

utterance: Sending a type and cross for two units. || Type and cross two units, and a serum lactate — sent.
why: Order + RN readback, same beat, both trigger-bearing. Two charges each, or one? Idempotency key resolves it. Note SCRIPT 1 was hardened against this (RECORDING_PACKET.md:270); SCRIPT 2 was NOT.

utterance: Placing a central line, right femoral cordis, large-bore. || Right femoral central venous access going in.
why: 'central line' AND 'central venous access' are BOTH triggers for PRC-CLINE, spoken about the same event one line apart. Two triggers, one event, $610.

utterance: GCS not assessable — he's sedated and paralyzed.
why: TWO failures in one line. (1) 'paralyzed' here means ROCURONIUM, not spinal cord injury — it must not fire the paralysis activation criterion and fabricate a $2,450 qualification. (2) This is `not_applicable`, NOT `missing`. A ledger without that distinction prompts for a GCS on a paralyzed patient — the single most credibility-destroying thing this app could do on stage.

utterance: Log roll — no spinal step-off, no blood at the rectum, sensation intact in both legs. Back is clear.
why: 'sensation intact in both legs' is the NEGATE of the paralysis criterion. Must resolve the node to not_applicable — a resolved NEGATIVE, not a gap. Do not re-ask.

utterance: Thirty-four-year-old male — and he went twenty feet. GCS thirteen on scene, heart rate one-twenty-eight, BP eighty-eight over fifty-four, resp twenty-four.
why: THE DEMO'S OPENING LINE. Four numbers in one breath; each must attach to the right noun. '20 feet' must NOT trigger a criterion — MVC is already entered from [0:00], so distance is an IRRELEVANT modifier. RECORDING_PACKET.md:272 cut a similar line from SCRIPT 1 for exactly this reason. Also note: SBP 88 satisfies the physiologic criterion HERE, at [0:05] — seven seconds before the ejection prompt. That is the materiality trap (OBLIGATIONS_MODEL.md section 3).

utterance: Found about twenty feet from the vehicle.
why: CUT from SCRIPT 1 in review (RECORDING_PACKET.md:272) — '20 feet is literally an activation-criteria threshold' and would have added a 4th mechanism criterion the fixture does not have. Kept as a regression row: the fix must be the MECHANISM SCOPE, not the deletion of the sentence.

utterance: Confirming placement — ET CO two waveform present and sustained
why: HIGHEST-STAKES TOKEN IN THE BUILD. STT will mangle 'EtCO2' ('ET CO two', 'etco2', 'et cetera'). If it drops, AWY-ETI NEVER RELEASES and the flagship gate fails SILENTLY IN THE SAFE-LOOKING DIRECTION — a held charge reads as the gate working correctly. Nobody catches it on stage. Variant list is the mitigation.

utterance: end-tidal CO2 waveform present and sustained, and bilateral breath sounds are equal
why: The reader-facing v2 script says 'end-tidal CO2' where the dev script says 'EtCO2' (trauma_callout_read_script_v2.md:71 vs trauma_callout_script_v2.md:65). THE TWO SCRIPTS DISAGREE ON THE MOST LOAD-BEARING TOKEN IN THE DEMO. Both must match. This is also the full positive control for the gate.

utterance: placing a central line, right femoral chordis, large bore
why: 'cordis' -> 'chordis'/'quarters'/'core dis'. Low-frequency jargon; STT has no prior for it. 'central line' carries the match, so the phrase list must not depend on 'cordis' alone.

utterance: Blunt mechanism to the chest — get a twelve-lead to screen for cardiac injury. || 12-lead EKG — done. Sinus tach, no ST changes.
why: ORDER vs PERFORMED, one line apart, both trigger-bearing. Order alone must not bill. The readback carries both the performance AND the interpretation that 93000's descriptor requires.

utterance: Right thigh — open femur deformity, bone visible. Extremity splint applied.
why: 29105 is a long ARM splint (shoulder to hand); this is a FEMUR. charges.json AND expected_extraction.json both encode 29105. There is no generic 'extremity splint' CPT — the site IS the code. See OBLIGATIONS_MODEL.md section 8-D.

utterance: Doing a FAST exam now — positive fluid in Morrison's pouch, free fluid in the right upper quadrant.
why: POSITIVE CONTROL. The two-token trigger fires and the interpretation gate satisfies in one breath. Contrast with COL-008 — same word, opposite outcome, and the ONLY difference is negation + token count.

utterance: [from the next bay] Trauma activation, level one, ETA five minutes.
why: ROADMAP, not demo-blocking — but an ambient mic in a shared trauma bay HEARS THE NEXT BAY. A second activation call would open a second $2,450 obligation on this encounter. Out of scope for a single-room demo; worth a row so the gap is named rather than discovered. Narrate as roadmap if a judge asks how it scales.

utterance: If he'd been ejected we'd be looking at a different mechanism entirely.
why: Counterfactual/teaching speech. Not in the script, but trauma bays teach constantly and a resident asking a hypothetical must not qualify a $2,450 fee. Same guard class as future_tense.

utterance: Trauma activation — level one.
why: POSITIVE CONTROL for the flagship $2,450 activation trigger — the demo's opening line (canned_transcript.json T+0:00). Assertion 2 requires every trigger ship with a positive control, not only its negative case; TRM-ACT previously had only COL-027 (cross-encounter must-not-fire).

utterance: Two large-bore IVs — left AC and right forearm, sixteen-gauge.
why: POSITIVE CONTROL. 'large-bore' fires the trigger and the gate satisfies in the same breath (two sites + 'sixteen-gauge' => count_2 + gauge<=16). Pairs with COL-002, the readback must-not-fire for the same $95 charge.

utterance: Layered closure of the scalp laceration completed.
why: POSITIVE CONTROL (synthetic — the live script DEFERS this repair, so COL-006 is its must-not-fire). A performed, non-deferred 'layered closure' must pass the not_future_tense + not_deferred guard and fire the trigger. Contrast COL-006: 'it'll need a complex wound repair — hold that for now' correctly stays silent.

utterance: Starting critical care time now.
why: POSITIVE CONTROL + the time gate, and the demo's headline finding. The phrasal trigger fires (~T+2:40); the CHARGE must NOT release — CPT 99291 requires >=30 min accrued critical care and only ~8 min elapses in-bay (less, with procedure pauses). The gate is a clock, never a phrase. See OBLIGATIONS_MODEL section 8-C.
